The power of localism can be subtle, but it can make a big difference at the right time. For example, during the 2008 U.S. presidential campaign, then-Senator Barack Obama's strategists devoted an unprecedented amount of money to the establishment of over 700 local field offices. They also decided not to staff those offices with external volunteers from other communities or states, as was often done in national campaigns, but instead to spend their funds to find and secure local volunteers to do intensive door-to-door get-out-the-vote duties within their own communities. Why? Because such neighbor-to-neighbor contact takes advantage of the persuasive power of localism, a political scientist's subsequent analysis of the effects of this local field office strategy indicated that it worked very well, winning the election for Obama in three closely contested battleground states, and, according to the author of the analysis, turning the national result from an electoral vote toss-up into an electoral vote blowout.
